question:
In a city, 85% of taxis are Green and 15% are Blue.

A witness identified the taxi as Blue. Witnesses correctly identify color 80% of the time.

What is the probability the taxi was actually Blue?
choices:
A) 50% — conflicting evidence makes it a toss-up
B) 80% — the witness is 80% accurate
C) 15% — that's the base rate for Blue taxis
D) About 41% — base rate dominates despite witness testimony

question:
A disease affects 1 in 10,000 people.

A test is 99% accurate (both sensitivity and specificity). You test positive.

What is the probability you actually have the disease?
choices:
A) 50% — the result is essentially random
B) 99% — the test is 99% accurate
C) 10,000 to 1 — the odds against having the disease
D) About 1% — false positives from healthy population vastly outnumber true positives

question:
You have two coins: one fair, one double-headed. You pick one at random and flip it 10 times. All 10 are heads.

What is the probability you picked the double-headed coin?
choices:
A) 50% — the coins were equally likely to be chosen initially
B) 10% — one in 10 chance for each head
C) About 99.9% — the double-headed coin is overwhelmingly more likely to produce 10 heads
D) 1 in 1024 — the probability a fair coin gives 10 heads

question:
A family has two children. You see one of them, a boy.

What's the probability the other is also a boy?
choices:
A) 2/3 — boys are more common than girls
B) 1/2 — the other child's gender is independent
C) 1/3 — given at least one boy, the possibilities are BB, BG, GB (not GG), so BB is 1/3
D) 1/4 — each combination (BB, BG, GB, GG) is equally likely

question:
A baseball player has an exceptional season, batting .400 (far above average).

What should you expect their batting average to be next season?
choices:
A) Even higher — they've reached a new level of skill
B) Exactly .400 again — performance is stable
C) Below average — exceptional seasons are followed by slumps
D) Closer to their career average — extreme performance tends to regress toward the mean

question:
Students who scored highest on a test received extra tutoring. Their next test scores were lower.

What explains this?
choices:
A) Regression to the mean — extremely high scores are partly luck and tend to decrease
B) The second test was more difficult
C) The tutoring was ineffective
D) The students became overconfident

question:
A forecaster consistently says they're 60% confident in predictions that turn out correct 80% of the time.

How would you describe their calibration?
choices:
A) Inconsistently calibrated — no pattern is discernible
B) Overconfident — 60% is too high for most predictions
C) Underconfident — their predictions are more reliable than their confidence suggests
D) Well-calibrated — confidence doesn't need to match accuracy exactly

question:
Students who are most confident about their answers tend to be less accurate than less confident students.

What does this paradox indicate?
choices:
A) The less confident students are actually more knowledgeable
B) This pattern is impossible — confidence and accuracy must correlate
C) Confidence is irrelevant to accuracy
D) Inverted calibration — confidence and accuracy are negatively correlated
